title: Chapter Thirty-Three: Six for Seven
content: I didn't fight the Council.

That was the secret. That was the stroke I'd been circling for three generations of women's blood and never quite reaching, because I'd been thinking like a Buena — like someone who had to *win* — when what I needed to do was something far simpler.
I needed to make them *leave*.

Cordelia's hex burned in my chest like a coal I'd swallowed whole. *What was taken in deceit returns sevenfold.* I'd been thinking of it as a weapon, a blade to drive into the Council's heart, but that was wrong. That was small. Cordelia hadn't built a weapon. She'd built a *collections policy*.
I pressed my palms flat against the earth between the Boundary Stones and spoke the hex into the ground.
Not at the Council. At the *land*.

"This is Buena territory," I said, and the words tasted like honey and copper — sweet and bloody, old and true. "This ground has been harvested without payment for two hundred and sixty years. Seven generations of theft. Seven generations of debt. I am calling the balance due."

For a heartbeat, nothing happened. The grey space the Council had spread across the forest floor sat there like a dead lake, flat and patient and certain of its own authority. The seven faceless figures inside it turned toward me with their smooth, featureless heads, and I felt the weight of their disdain — the contempt of an institution so old it had forgotten what it was like to be challenged.
Then the ground *rebelled*.

It started as a tremor, barely perceptible, the kind of thing you'd dismiss as a truck on a distant highway. But it wasn't a truck. It was the land remembering what it was. It was the soil rejecting a tenant who had never paid rent. It was two hundred and sixty years of accumulated grievance rising up through bedrock and clay and root, and the grey space — that suffocating, bureaucratic nowhere the Council had pulled over the forest like a filing cabinet — *buckled*.
The ground spat them out.

I watched it happen and I could not breathe for the beauty of it. The grey space collapsed inward, folding like wet paper, and the seven faceless figures were *dragged* — physically, bodily dragged — out of their jurisdiction and into mine. They tumbled across the Boundary Stones' boundary like tenants evicted at midnight, their grey nothing-space peeling away from them in strips and ribbons, and underneath —
Underneath, they had bodies.

Not human bodies. Not anymore. But *forms*. Limbs, torsos, the suggestion of heads that had once worn faces before they'd traded them for authority. Seven figures standing at the seven stones, blinking in the moonlight like creatures dragged from a cave into sudden sun. The air around them rippled with displaced ozone, sharp and electric, the smell of a system crash.
For the first time in two hundred and sixty years, the Hollow Council occupied physical space in a world they did not control.
And in that world, the dead could speak.
They came like a flood.

I felt them before I saw them — the temperature dropping, the pressure building, the forest holding its breath. Then the five remaining ancestresses manifested all at once, not as flickering shadows or distant voices but as *themselves*, solid and blazing and *furious*. Cordelia with her ledger-dark eyes. Iris trailing sparks like a comet's tail. Hazel covered in mirrors that reflected nothing but truth. Vivienne crackling with stolen seconds. Nadine, patient Nadine, already reaching for threads of light only she could see.
Seven women on one side. Seven faceless bureaucrats on the other.
The math had never been so beautiful.
But it was Maren who moved first.

Maren, who had been silenced. Maren, whose voice the Council had bound with their first and cruelest law — the one that said the dead could not testify against the living, that the murdered could not accuse their murderers, that two hundred and sixty years of evidence could be dismissed because the witnesses were technically deceased.
Maren opened her mouth, and the forest *shook*.
"WE ARE HERE."

Her voice hit like thunder rolled flat — not the rolling rumble of a distant storm but the crack directly overhead, the boom that rattles your teeth and vibrates in your sternum. The Boundary Stones hummed with it. The trees bent like reeds. The seven faceless figures staggered backward, and I saw — I *saw* — the place where their faces should have been ripple with something like alarm.
"WE HAVE ALWAYS BEEN HERE."

The second wave was louder. Not volume — *conviction*. Two hundred and sixty years of silence compressed into six words and detonated. I tasted honey and fire on my tongue, the sweetness of a voice too long denied and the rage of its finally being freed. My eyes watered. My ears rang. I could smell the ozone sharpening, that electric-silver scent of magic being worked at scale, and underneath it the green pulse of living things recognizing their own.
"AND WE WILL NOT BE SILENCED AGAIN."
The third strike sealed it.

Maren's Voice-Binding — the gift they'd murdered her to suppress — wove through the air like visible sound, strands of deep violet light wrapping around the space between the stones, closing doors that the Council hadn't even known were open. They couldn't retreat. Couldn't dissolve into their grey nowhere. Couldn't file a jurisdictional appeal or invoke a procedural delay or do any of the bureaucratic evasions they'd relied on for centuries. The space was sealed. The hearing was in session.
The first wound had been silence. The first weapon was voice.
And then — *then* — the symphony began.

Cordelia moved first, because foundations come before everything. She pressed her hands against the nearest stone, and the hex she'd written in 1794 — the one I'd carried in my chest like a coal — *bloomed*. The Prosperity Hex didn't just glow; it *funded*. Amber light poured from the stone's surface like liquid capital, like blood money returning to its source, and every grain of power the Council had siphoned from Buena land for two hundred and sixty years began flowing back into the circle. The stones drank it. The ground drank it. The barrier that had always been underfunded, under-resourced, built on prayers and pocket change, suddenly had *backing*.

Iris came next. She always came next — fire after fuel, spark after wood. The amber light that Cordelia had invested needed power, and Iris had been burning for two centuries. She ignited like a paper lantern, crimson and gold, and her fire ran along the strands Cordelia had laid down and *charged* them. The air in the circle went from warm to hot to kiln-heat, pressing against my skin like I was standing inside a furnace, and I was sweating and gasping and *grinning* because this was what the barrier was supposed to feel like. This was what it felt like when it was *funded* and *powered* and not just a desperate prayer held together by stubbornness and spite.

Hazel's illusions unfurled like smoke from a sigil. She wove them around the seven Council members with the practiced hand of a woman who'd spent her living life making people see what she wanted them to see — and now, in death, making them *not* see. Each faceless figure found themselves in a separate pocket of confusion, unable to perceive their colleagues, unable to coordinate. Seven individuals fighting seven battles instead of one united front. The Council had always been terrifying because it acted as one; Hazel made it seven, and seven is just a number.

Vivienne accelerated time. It was the cruelest gift and the most poetic — centuries of debt coming due in seconds. The Council had operated on geological patience, thinking in decades and centuries, confident that no human institution could match their timeline. Vivienne crashed their schedule. I watched the faceless figures twitch and stagger as two hundred and sixty years of accumulated obligation hit them all at once — every unpaid debt, every unacknowledged theft, every year of stolen vitality crystallizing into a single overdue bill. Their forms flickered. Their edges frayed. They were *aging*, not like flesh ages but like institutions age when their legitimacy crumbles — cracks in the foundation, rot in the beams, the accumulated weight of every lie they'd ever told pressing down on them at once.
And Nadine — patient, methodical Nadine, who had waited forty years to begin weaving because she'd known the pattern would take more than one lifetime — Nadine smiled.

She reached into the air and pulled out threads of amber light, and she began to *weave*. Not carefully, not tentatively, but with the sure hands of a woman who had mapped every strand before she ever touched the loom. The strands ran from stone to stone to stone, crossing and recrossing, and I saw the pattern — I *recognized* it — the same double-helix structure I'd seen in my dreams, the barrier that wasn't a wall but a *song*, a frequency, a harmonic that would resonate through every layer of reality and bounce the Council's grey nothing out of Buena land forever.

The heat was extraordinary. Standing at the center of seven gifts converging was like standing in the heart of a star, and I felt it on every inch of my skin — the searing certainty of Cordelia's justice, the fierce joy of Iris's fire, the cool precision of Hazel's illusions cutting like scalpels, the dizzying rush of Vivienne's temporal acceleration, the patient rightness of Nadine's weaving, and Maren's voice — Maren's *voice* — still ringing through the circle like a bell that had been struck and would never stop ringing.
The Council was losing.

I could see it in the way their forms destabilized, grey edges flaking away like ash, the bureaucratic certainty that had armored them for centuries cracking and falling in pieces. They tried to rally — one of them raised what might have been a hand and shouted in a voice like a closing file drawer: "The contract was signed! Seven generations! The terms are CLEAR!"
The words hit the circle and dissolved into static.
Another figure tried: "Authority was granted! The signatory had standing! The agreement is BINDING!"

Maren turned toward them, and I watched the last Buena matriarch — the woman whose throat they'd cut to enforce their first silence — fix her gaze on the Council with two hundred and sixty years of accumulated fury.

"The contract was signed in bad faith," she said. Her voice wasn't thunder anymore. It was worse. It was *quiet* — the kind of quiet that makes the air itself listen. "The signatory did not have authority to trade what was not his. A husband cannot sell his wife's inheritance. A father cannot barter his daughter's birthright. And a man who murders his business partner to claim their shared property has *no standing* to invoke the terms of that partnership."
She took a step forward. The Council recoiled.

"We have two hundred and sixty years of evidence," Maren said. "Every journal. Every ledger. Every whispered story passed from mother to daughter in the dark. Every death you caused. Every life you consumed. Every lie you told to maintain a contract that was void before the ink dried. The original signatory committed fraud. The subsequent enforcement constitutes theft. And you — all of you — have been operating under a void instrument for over two and a half centuries."

The faceless figures converged. For the first time, they looked small. Not human — never human — but *diminished*. The weight of bad faith was settling on them like sediment, like ash, like the accumulated debris of every lie they'd ever enforced.
"Under whose jurisdiction?" one of them demanded, but the voice was thin now, reedy, the voice of a clerk whose filing system has just been declared unconstitutional.
Maren smiled. It was not a kind smile.
"Ours," she said. "We are the injured party. We have always been the injured party. And in this space, at this hour, at last — *at last* — we have standing."
I stood at the center of the circle and watched the barrier rise.

Strands of amber light wove between the seven stones in the double-helix pattern Nadine had mapped and Cordelia had funded and Iris had powered, and it was *beautiful* — the most beautiful thing I had ever seen. It gleamed like DNA, like music made visible, like a frequency that could shatter grey space and let the light back in. The Council was sealed inside, trapped behind walls woven from their own stolen power, and for the first time since 1764 they were experiencing what it felt like to be on the wrong side of an unbreakable agreement.
Six ghosts. One living girl. Seven generations.
The ritual was working.

The taste in my mouth was honey and fire — victory and rage braided together so tightly I couldn't tell where one ended and the other began. I could smell the ozone of seven gifts working in concert, sharp and electric, and underneath it the green breath of forest reclaiming its own. I could see the amber light weaving and crossing, the most intricate pattern any Buena had ever attempted, every strand placed with the precision of two centuries of preparation. I could hear Maren's voice still resonating through the stones, the seal she'd sung still holding, the space still closed. I could feel the heat of convergence pressing against my skin like I was standing inside a kiln, inside a star, inside the heart of something that had been waiting two hundred and sixty years to ignite.
And then I felt something else.

A cold thread cutting through the heat. A wrongness approaching through the burning forest, moving with the deliberate purpose of something that had never been meant to stop. I knew that feeling. I'd felt it in the cemetery when the earth opened. I'd felt it in my dreams when the curse showed me its endgame.
Julian was coming.

The barrier needed one more strand. I could see it in the pattern — the gap where the seventh thread should weave, the empty channel waiting to be filled. Six ancestress gifts ringed the circle, burning and weaving and binding and accelerating and hexing and speaking, but the seventh position stood open. The living position. The position that couldn't be filled by a gift, because gifts were what the Council had been consuming. The seventh strand had to be something they couldn't take.
A choice.

The barrier needed me. Not my gift — I didn't have one, not in the way they did. The seventh piece of the pattern was a *decision*, freely made, uncoerced, unbound by contract or coercion or curse. A living woman choosing to stand in the circle and complete what her ancestresses began. A living woman choosing to be the last thread in a pattern that had been weaving since before she was born.

Julian was coming through the forest, the curse's final instrument, the blade they'd forged in my brother's shape. If I stepped into the seventh position, I'd be anchored here. Unable to run. Unable to hide. Facing whatever the Council's last desperate weapon could throw at me.
But if I didn't, the barrier would have a hole in it. A gap. A contract with an escape clause.
Six ancestresses. One living girl. Seven generations.
The math had never been clearer.
I stepped forward into the empty strand.
